Name of candidates from Registered but Unrecognized Political Party In which order the categories of candidates in form 7A will be arranged from top to bottom?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **As per Section 38 of Representative of People Act, 1951 the Publication of list of contesting candidates shall be classified as: (i) candidates of recognised political parties; (ii) candidates of registered political parties other than those mentioned in clause (i); (iii) other candidates. While Form 7A mentioned under the Conduct of Election rules the contesting candidates will be arranged based on section 38 of RPA, 1951 as follows:**II. Names of candidates from Recognized National or State Party**(first the names of such candidates from these parties will be published)**III. Name of candidates from Registered but Unrecognized Political Party**(at second position the names of such candidates will be published).**I: Names of candidates from Independent Party** (At the end names of such candidates will be published)

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to India, consider the following pairs:`nAction The Act under which it is covered
1. Unauthorized wearing of police or military uniforms The Official Secrets Act, 1923
2. Knowingly misleading or otherwise interfering with a police officer or military officer when engaged in their duties The Indian Evidence Act, 1872
3. Celebratory gunfire which can endanger the safety of others The Arms (Amendment) Act 2019
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to India, consider the following pairs:`nAction The Act under which it is covered 1. Unauthorized wearing of police or military uniforms The Official Secrets Act, 1923 2. Knowingly misleading or otherwise interfering with a police officer or military officer when engaged in their duties The Indian Evidence Act, 1872 3. Celebratory gunfire which can endanger the safety of others The Arms (Amendment) Act 2019 How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **

Pair 1 is correctly matched**: The Official Secrets Act, 1923 prohibits person from using or wearing, without lawful authority, any naval, military, air force, police or other official uniform, or any uniform so nearly resembling the same as to be calculated to deceive, or falsely represents himself to be a person who is or has been entitled to use or wear any such uniform**Pair 2 is incorrectly matched**: It is under The Official Secrets Act, 1923, that no person in the vicinity of any prohibited place shall obstruct,**knowingly mislead or otherwise interfere with **or impede,**any police officer, or any member of **the Armed Forces of the Union engaged on guard, sentry, patrol or other similar duty in relation to the prohibited place.**

Pair 3 is correctly matched**: The Arms Act Amendment of 2019 adds new offences such as forcefully taking a firearm from police or armed forces and**using firearms in a celebratory gunfire **which endangers human life or personal safety of others.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: In context of drafting of constitution of India, Munshi-Ayyangar formula is related to:
questions.questionText: In context of drafting of constitution of India, Munshi-Ayyangar formula is related to:

options.a: Deciding the subjects under the various lists of Schedule VII.
options.b: Method of representation of states in the upper house of the Parliament.
options.c: Deciding the official language of the Union government.
options.d: Method of reorganisation of Indian states after independenc

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **In the Constituent Assembly, deciding the official language of India was one of the key debates. The main questions were about the type of script and numerals to be used in the official communication of the union government. Hindustani (Hindi with more Urdu elements) and Sanskrit were among those proposed as official languages.**As part of this debate, the Constitution assembly adopted the Munshi-Ayyangar formula**as a c**ompromise solution **to decide the official language of the union. **The formula was named after Drafting Committee members K M Munshi and N Gopalaswamy Ayyangar. As an outcome of the Munshi-Ayyangar formula, Article 343 of the Constitution was adopted in 1950, with the provisions that-a) "The official language of the Union shall be Hindi in Devanagari script." b) "The form of numerals to be used for the official purposes of the Union shall be the international form of Indian numerals." c) "for a period of fifteen years from the commencement of this Constitution, the English language shall continue to be used for all the official purposes of the Union for which it was being used immediately before such commencement."

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to the Committee of Parliament on Official Language constituted under the Official Languages Act of 1963 consider the following statements:
I. Its members are elected by the members of the respective houses of the Parliament in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of a single transferable vote.
II. The Union Home Minister is the ex-officio chairman of the committee.
III. It is the duty of the Committee to review the progress made in the use of Hindi for the official purpose of the Union and to submit a report to the President.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Committee of Parliament on Official Language constituted under the Official Languages Act of 1963 consider the following statements: I. Its members are elected by the members of the respective houses of the Parliament in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of a single transferable vote. II. The Union Home Minister is the ex-officio chairman of the committee. III. It is the duty of the Committee to review the progress made in the use of Hindi for the official purpose of the Union and to submit a report to the President. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **As per section 4 of the Official Language Act, 1963 after the expiry of ten years from the date on which section 3 of the Act comes into force (26th January, 1965), a Committee of Parliament on Official Language shall be constituted, on passing of a resolution to that effect being moved in either of the Houses of Parliament with previous sanction of the President and passed by both the Houses. 

Statement I is correct: The Committee consist of 30 members of whom 20 shall be from the Lok Sabha and 10 from **the Rajya Sabha to be elected respectively by the members of the Lok Sabha and the members of Rajya Sabha in **accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of a single transferable vote. **

Statement II is incorrect: **The committee is currently chaired by the Union Home Minister. The Union Minister of Home Affairs from time to time** has by convention, **been elected as the Chairmen of the Committee by its members.**There is no provision that the Union Home minister will be the ex-officio chairman of the committee. **

Statement III is correct: **According to the provisions of the Official Languages Act 1963,**the duties entrusted to the **Committee are to review the progress made in the use of Hindi for the official purposes of the Union and to submit **a report to the President**while making recommendations on it. The President will get that report laid before every House of Parliament thereafter and send it to all the State Governments.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to the provisions given under Constitution of India about the official languages for various offices, consider the following statements:
I. The official language of the Union shall be Hindi in Devanagari script.
II. All proceedings in the Supreme Court and High Courts are to be conducted only in English.
III. The Governor of a state is empowered to decide the official language of the respective state.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the provisions given under Constitution of India about the official languages for various offices, consider the following statements: I. The official language of the Union shall be Hindi in Devanagari script. II. All proceedings in the Supreme Court and High Courts are to be conducted only in English. III. The Governor of a state is empowered to decide the official language of the respective state. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct: As per Article 343(1) of the constitution of India, Hindi in Devanagari script is designated as **Official language of the Union. **

Statement II is incorrect: According to Article 348(1)a) of the Constitution of India, all proceedings in the Supreme **Court and in every High Court are to be conducted in the English language. However, Article 348(2) of the **Constitution of India states that the Governor of a State with the previous **consent of the President can authorise the use of the Hindi language, or any other language used for any official purposes of the State in proceedings in**the **High Court having its principal seat in that State. **

Statement III is incorrect: Article 345 of the Constitution of India empowers the State Legislature(not Governor of **the State) by law to adopt any one or more of the languages **currently in use within the State, or Hindi, as the language(s) to be used for all or any of the official purposes of the State.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: Consider the following Indian languages:
I. Odia
II. Marathi
III. Bengali
IV. Prakrit
V. Tulu
How many of the above languages are given the status of classical language by the Government of India?
questions.questionText: Consider the following Indian languages: I. Odia II. Marathi III. Bengali IV. Prakrit V. Tulu How many of the above languages are given the status of classical language by the Government of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Classical languages are defined by their ancient origins and their contribution to the intellectual, philosophical, and religious evolution of India. In 2004, the Government of India introduced the "Classical Languages" (Shastriya Bhasha) category to recognize languages with deep historical roots, rich literary traditions, and distinct cultural significance. The Indian government announced in 2004 that languages might hold the title of "Classical Language" of India if they fulfilled a number of certain requirements. Odia was given the classical language status in 2014, Further Marathi, Prakrit and Bengali were awarded the classical language status in 2024.**However, Tulu is not designated as the **classical language by the Union government. **

Knowledge Base: **In 2004, the Government of India, for the first time, created a new category of languages known as Classical Languages. The criteria was revised in 2024 based on the recommendations of the Linguistic Experts Committees (LEC) under Sahitya Akademi. The revised criteria include: High antiquity of early texts, and recorded history over a period of**1500-20**00 years; A body of ancient literature/ texts, which is considered a heritage by generations of speakers; Knowledge texts, especially prose texts in addition to poetry; and epigraphic and inscriptional evidence;
That classical languages and literature could be distinct from their current form or could be discontinuous with later forms of its offshoots.
The Ministry of Home Affairs initially granted the status to Tamil and Sanskrit, and the Ministry of Culture took over the responsibility for further implementations and future recognition.
So far, eleven languages have been recognized as classical languages: Tamil (2004), Sanskrit (2005), Kannada (2008), Telugu (2008), Malayalam (2013), and Odia (2014). In 2024, five additional languages received this recognition: Marathi, Pali, Prakrit, Assamese, and Bengali.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: The Ninety-Seventh Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011 brought changes to which of the following parts of the Indian constitution?
I. Part III
II. Part IV
III. Part VII
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: The Ninety-Seventh Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011 brought changes to which of the following parts of the Indian constitution? I. Part III II. Part IV III. Part VII Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The Ninety-Seventh Constitutional Amendment of 2011 gave cooperative societies constitutional recognition. It added Part IXB (Articles 243-ZH to 243-ZT) to the Constitution providing for a detailed framework for the registration, regulation, and winding up of co-operatives and brought changes in few parts of the Constitution of India.** Option I is correct:**The Ninety-Seventh Amendment of 2011 added the right to form co-operatives as a **Fundamental Right**under Article 19(1)c) of Part III of the Constitution of India.** Option II is correct:**A new Article 43B**was inserted into**Part IV (Directive Principles of State Policy)**of the Constitution through the**Ninety-Seventh Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011**, directing the State to promote the voluntary formation, autonomous functioning, democratic control, and professional management of cooperative societies.** Option III is incorrect:**Part VII**of the constitution of India was related to States in Part B of the First Schedule. It was**completely repealed by the 7th Constitutional Amendment (1956) and has no relation to The Ninety-Seventh **Amendment of 2011.**

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to Home Guards, consider the following statements:
1. Home Guards are raised under the Home Guards Act and Rules of the Central Government.
2. The role of the Home Guards is to serve as an auxiliary force to the police in maintenance of internal security.
3. To prevent infiltration on the international border/coastal areas, the Border Wing Home Guards Battalions have been raised in some States.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Home Guards, consider the following statements: 1. Home Guards are raised under the Home Guards Act and Rules of the Central Government. 2. The role of the Home Guards is to serve as an auxiliary force to the police in maintenance of internal security. 3. To prevent infiltration on the international border/coastal areas, the Border Wing Home Guards Battalions have been raised in some States. How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**'Home Guards' is a voluntary force, first raised in India in December 1946, to assist the police in controlling civil disturbance and communal riots.**

Statement 1 is incorrect:**Home Guards are raised under the Home Guards Act and Rules of the States/Union Territories (not central Government). They are recruited from amongst all classes of people and walks of life, who give their spare time to the organisation for betterment of the community.**

Statement 2 is correct:**The role of Home Guards is to serve as an auxiliary Force to the Police in maintenance of internal security situations, help the community in any kind of emergency such as an air-raid, fire, cyclone, earthquake, epidemic etc., help in maintenance of essential services, promote communal harmony and assist the administration in protecting weaker sections, participate in socio-economic and welfare activities and perform Civil Defence duties.**

Statement 3 is correct:**Fifteen Border Wing Home Guards (BWHG) Battalions have been raised in the border States viz. Punjab (6 Bns.), Rajasthan ( 4 Bns.), Gujarat (2 Bns.) and one each Battalion for Meghalaya, Tripura and West Bengal to serve as an auxiliary to Border Security Force for preventing infiltration on the international border/coastal areas, guarding of VA/VPs and lines of communication in vulnerable areas at the time of external aggression.**Therefore, only two statements are correct.**

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With respect to the general elections to the Lok Sabha, once the result of all the constituencies are declared, which authority will constitute the new Lok Sabha by notifying in the official gazette the names of the elected members to the House?
questions.questionText: With respect to the general elections to the Lok Sabha, once the result of all the constituencies are declared, which authority will constitute the new Lok Sabha by notifying in the official gazette the names of the elected members to the House?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer **According to Sec. 73 of the Representative of People Act,1951, after the results of all Parliamentary constituencies **are declared, the Election Commission of India will constitute the new Lok Sabha by notifying in the official **gazette the names of the elected members. **

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: In the context of the elections in India, what is the primary responsibility of a Booth Level Officer (BLO)?
questions.questionText: In the context of the elections in India, what is the primary responsibility of a Booth Level Officer (BLO)?

options.a: To supervise the functioning of polling stations on the day of voting.
options.b: To conduct verification of EVMs and VVPATs before polling.
options.c: To manage training arrangements for polling staff.
options.d: To maintain and authenticate the electoral roll at the booth level.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. A**Booth Level Officer (BLO)**acts as the Election Commission's local field representative. The BLO's primary duty is to**maintain, verify, and update the electoral roll**for their assigned area. Their primary tasks include: Adding new voters to the constituency Processing deletions due to death, relocation, or other reasons Special Revision of the Voter List Distribution of Voter ID Cards Assisting people with the procedures related to Form 6, Form 7, and Form 8 This foundational task directly supports the**smooth and fair conduct of elections**, as accurate voter lists are essential for ensuring transparency and preventing irregularities.**

Option a is incorrect.**On polling day, t**he responsibilities at polling stations lie with polling staff under the **supervision of the Returning Officer (RO) for the constituency. BLO's main role is pre**poll**(roll maintenance, voterlist verification). On polling day, they may help inform or mobilise voters, but supervision and management of the polling station is not their duty.
**Option b is incorrect.** The verification, testing and sealing of Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs) and Voterverified Paper Audit Trail (VVPAT) is a **technical and polling**station level function, typically handled by polling staff under **RO/DEO oversight and not the BLO**. BLOs are not trained or tasked for EVM/VVPAT operations. Their main mandate is voterlist maintenance.**

Option c is incorrect**. The** training of polling staff is organised by higher election authorities, typically the District **Election Officer (DEO) or RO, with support from the state's Chief Electoral Officer (CEO)**. BLOs are not responsible for training polling staff. Their role remains administrative for electoral rolls and voter interface.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to the Commissioner for Linguistic Minorities in India (CLM), consider the following statements:
I. S/he is appointed by the President of India. II The office of the Commissioner for Linguistic Minorities in India (CLM) is under the administrative control of the Ministry of Home Affairs.
III. S/he investigates all matters relating to the safeguards provided for linguistic minorities under the Constitution and reports to the President upon those matters.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Commissioner for Linguistic Minorities in India (CLM), consider the following statements: I. S/he is appointed by the President of India. II The office of the Commissioner for Linguistic Minorities in India (CLM) is under the administrative control of the Ministry of Home Affairs. III. S/he investigates all matters relating to the safeguards provided for linguistic minorities under the Constitution and reports to the President upon those matters. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**.**Article 350B**, inserted through the**7th Constitutional Amendment (1956)**, creates the institution of the**Special **Officer for Linguistic Minorities**, whose mandate is to safeguard the interests of linguistic minority communities.**

Statement I is correct**. Article**350B(1)**of the Constitution provides for the appointment of a**Special Officer for **Linguistic Minorities (**who is known as the Commissioner for Linguistic Minorities in India (CLM))** by the President of **India**. This method of appointment underlines the**importance accorded**to linguistic rights and ensures a measure of**functional autonomy**for the office.**

Statement II is incorrect**.**The office of**Commissioner for Linguistic Minorities**is **under the purview of the**Ministry **of Minority Affairs**and not under the purview of the Ministry of Home Affairs.**

Statement III is correct**. Article**350B(2)**sets out the responsibilities of the Special Officer. It requires the Officer to**review how constitutional safeguards for linguistic minorities are being implemented**in different States. The Officer must then**submit the reports in this regard to the President at intervals specified by the President**. These reports are subsequently laid before**both Houses of Parliament**and forwarded to the concerned State Governments. This system ensures continuous oversight and enables corrective action wherever required.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to election petitions in the case of a parliamentary constituency for general elections to the House of People, consider the following statements:
I. Such petitions fall under the original jurisdiction of the High court within whose territorial jurisdiction of the concerned constituency is located.
II. Such a petition can be only filed by a candidate in such elections.
III. Such a petition must be filed within forty-five days from the date of declaration of the election results.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to election petitions in the case of a parliamentary constituency for general elections to the House of People, consider the following statements: I. Such petitions fall under the original jurisdiction of the High court within whose territorial jurisdiction of the concerned constituency is located. II. Such a petition can be only filed by a candidate in such elections. III. Such a petition must be filed within forty-five days from the date of declaration of the election results. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. Election petitions are the statutory mechanism for questioning the validity of results declared in**Parliamentary, **Assembly, and local elections**. The procedure is governed primarily by the**Representation of the People Act, 1951. **

Statement I is correct**. In Lok Sabha elections, disputes relating to the validity of the result can be challenged only through an**election petition**. Under**Section 80A of the Representation of the People Act, 1951**,**original jurisdiction **regarding hearing of such petitions **lies with the**High Court**of the State where the elections are concluded.**

Statement II is incorrect**. The right to file an election petition is laid down under**Section 81(1)**of the RPA, 1951. It states that an election petition may be presented by:**any candidate**at the election, or**any elector**of the constituency. An "elector" is defined under**Section 2(e)**of the Act as a person entitled to vote, regardless of whether they actually voted. This provision ensures broad standing so that both contestants and voters may legally challenge a disputed election. Thus, only the candidate in such an election does not have an exclusive right to file an election petition.**

Statement III is correct**. The time period for filing an election petition is specified in**Section 81(1)**of the RPA, 1951. It mandates that a petition must be filed**within forty-five days from the date of election of the returned candidate. **In practice, this means**forty-five days from the date the results are officially declared**. This statutory deadline ensures that election disputes are raised promptly and resolved without delay.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to Indian Polity, consider the following statements:
Statement I: It is obligatory for the President to give his/her assent to a Constitutional Amendment bill passed by the Parliament.
Statement II: A Constitutional Amendment Bill can be introduced in Parliament only with a prior recommendation from the President.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: With reference to Indian Polity, consider the following statements: Statement I: It is obligatory for the President to give his/her assent to a Constitutional Amendment bill passed by the Parliament. Statement II: A Constitutional Amendment Bill can be introduced in Parliament only with a prior recommendation from the President. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I.
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct.
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct:**Under**Article 368(2)**, a Constitutional Amendment Bill can be introduced in**either House of **Parliament**by a minister or a private member, and it must be passed in each House by**a majority of the total **membership**, and a**two-thirds majority of members present and voting**. Any Amendments that affect federal provisions must be**ratified by at least one-half of the State Legislatures**. After the** Constitution (Twenty-fourth Amendment) Act, 1971**, Article 368 was amended to state that**when such a Bill **is presented to the President, "he shall give his assent."**Thus, the President**cannot withhold or return**a Constitutional Amendment Bill.**

Statement II is not correct: A Constitutional Amendment Bill does not require the prior permission or **recommendation of the President for its introduction in Parliament**. Under Article 368(2), an amendment to the Constitution may be initiated by introducing a Bill in either House of Parliament, and any Member of Parliament-whether a minister or a private member-may introduce it without needing the President's recommendation.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: Which one of the following statements most appropriately defines the term "Separate Electorate" introduced in India during the British rule in India? For electing a representative from a particular community, the candidate must belong to that community:
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements most appropriately defines the term "Separate Electorate" introduced in India during the British rule in India? For electing a representative from a particular community, the candidate must belong to that community:

options.a: And all the voters in the constituency can cast their votes to select the candidate.
options.b: But only the male voters in the constituency would be eligible to cast their vote to select the candidate.
options.c: But only those voters would be eligible to vote to select the candidate who belongs to that community.
options.d: But only those voters would be eligible to vote to select the candidate who are above a certain income threshold.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The British Government formally introduced**Separate Electorates**through the Indian Councils Act, 1909 and expanded them under the Government of India Act of 1919 and 1935. Under this system,**only voters belonging to a **particular religious or social community were placed on that community's separate electoral roll**and**only **candidates belonging to the same community could contest**from that separate electorate. Thus,**each community **elected its representative exclusively through its own voters**,**without participation from members of other **communities**.**

Option a is incorrect.**All voters in a constituency may vote, but only candidates belonging to a designated **community can contest from that constituency. **This describes a**reserved constituency**, not a separate electorate. In reserved seats, the candidate is restricted by the community, but the voters are not.**

Option b is incorrect.**A separate electorate does not only include the male voters in the constituency.**

Option d is incorrect.**Income threshold was a criteria with respect to voting during the British rule in India but is **not related to a separate electorate.**

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to the electoral process in India, consider the following statements about "None of the Above (NOTA)":
I. NOTA option is not available to the members of the electoral college in case of Presidential elections.
II. NOTA button is the first option on the voting panel of EVM
III. If NOTA gets maximum votes in a constituency, re-elections take place in that constituency.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the electoral process in India, consider the following statements about "None of the Above (NOTA)": I. NOTA option is not available to the members of the electoral college in case of Presidential elections. II. NOTA button is the first option on the voting panel of EVM III. If NOTA gets maximum votes in a constituency, re-elections take place in that constituency. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The "None of the Above" (NOTA) option was introduced in India to give voters the power to express their dissatisfaction with all candidates in an election. The Supreme Court in the People's Union for Civil Liberties (PUCL) vs Union of India case 2013 ruled that voter secrecy must be maintained for all, emphasising its importance for fair elections. The Court also directed the Election Commission of India to add a NOTA (None of the Above) button to EVMs, to reduce bogus voting and promote integrity in candidates.**

Statement I is correct**: The system of the NOTA option began after a directive from the Supreme Court in 2013. The option, however, is meant for direct elections, and t**hus not applicable to the Presidential elections. Thus the **members of the electoral college do not have a NOTA option in case of Presidential elections.**

Statement II is incorrect:**After the Supreme Court order in September 2013, the Election Commission added the **NOTA button on the EVMs as the last option on the voting panel. **

Statement III is incorrect: **. In India, re-election does not take place in the instance where NOTA gets maximum votes. Instead, the candidate with the second-highest valid votes is declared the winner.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to the electoral process in India, consider the following statements about Exit Polls:

I. The conduct of exit polls is prohibited in case of a general election to the House of people from the hour fixed for commencement of polls in the first phase and till half an hour after the time fixed for close of poll for the last phase in all the States.
II. No exit polls can be published by any newspaper or new channels under any circumstance during the voting period.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the electoral process in India, consider the following statements about Exit Polls: I. The conduct of exit polls is prohibited in case of a general election to the House of people from the hour fixed for commencement of polls in the first phase and till half an hour after the time fixed for close of poll for the last phase in all the States. II. No exit polls can be published by any newspaper or new channels under any circumstance during the voting period. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Exit polls are surveys conducted by authorised agencies that collect information from voters**immediately after they **cast their vote and exit the polling station**. They aim to estimate**voting patterns, preferred choices of voters, and **demographic trends**, based on responses voluntarily provided by voters. Exit polls are**not official results**but are statistical predictions of the likely electoral outcome.**

Statement I is correct**: In India, the regulation of exit**polls is primarily governed by Section 126A of the **Representation of the People Act, 1951. This provision prohibits the conduct, publication, or dissemination of exit **poll results during the period notified by the Election Commission of India to prevent undue influence on voters **and maintain the integrity of the electoral process. **The time period notified during a general election is the hour fixed for commencement of polls in the first phase **and half an hour after the time fixed for close of poll for the last phase in all the States. **

Statement II is correct: **The Election Commission of India (ECI) oversees the regulations surrounding exit polls.** The **ECI has issued guidelines under Article 324 of the Constitution, which forbid newspapers and news channels from **publishing pre-election surveys and exit poll results during the voting period. **

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to the recent election to the House of People in India, consider the following hypothetical scenario: A registered Political Party has:
I. won 2 Lok Sabha seats from State A, where the total allocated seats for the state in the House is 2 seats.
II. secured more than 8% of the votes polled in State A.

III. won 1 Lok Sabha seat from State B, where the total allocated seat for the state in the house is 80 seats and has secured 5% of total valid votes polled in the State. Based on the above performance of the political party, what type of recognition could be given by the Election Commission of India to the party as per the Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968?
questions.questionText: With reference to the recent election to the House of People in India, consider the following hypothetical scenario: A registered Political Party has: I. won 2 Lok Sabha seats from State A, where the total allocated seats for the state in the House is 2 seats. II. secured more than 8% of the votes polled in State A. III. won 1 Lok Sabha seat from State B, where the total allocated seat for the state in the house is 80 seats and has secured 5% of total valid votes polled in the State. Based on the above performance of the political party, what type of recognition could be given by the Election Commission of India to the party as per the Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968?

options.a: State Party status in State A only
options.b: State Party status in States A and B only
options.d: National Party status and State Party status in both the states

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer **The recognition of political parties in India is governed by the**Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, **1968**, issued under the powers of the**Election Commission of India (ECI)**. The relevant provisions are found under**Paragraph 6A**for State Party status and**Paragraph 6B**for National Party status of the act. In the hypothetical scenario,**Condition I is correct. The political party has secured 100% of the seats allocated to the state in the Lok Sabha by **winning 2 Lok Sabha seats out of the 2 total allotted seats. **This fulfills the condition unde**r Paragraph 6A of the **Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968-At the last general election to the House of the People **from the State, the party returned at least one member to the House of the People for every 25 members. Hence, it **can be recognised as a State Political Party by the Election Commission of India in State A. **For eg **In Arunachal Pradesh, 2 seats are allotted for elections to the House of People in the state. If a party wins both seats, it will be eligible to be granted State Political Party Status in Arunachal Pradesh by the ECI.**Condition II is correct**. The political party fulfils the condition mentioned in**Paragraph 6A of the Election Symbols **(Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968 **by securing** not less than eight per cent of the total valid votes polled in **the State at the last general election to the House of the People from the State A. Hence, it fulfils another condition **to be recognised as a State Political Party Status in State A. **Condition III is incorrect. **The Political Party has won 1 Lok Sabha seat from State B where the total allocated seat for the state in the house is 80 seats and this does not qualify the condition -**At the last general election to the House of **the People from the State, the party has returned at least one member to the House of the People for every 25 **members, mentioned in Paragraph 6A of **the**Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968 OR **At the last general election to the House of the People from that State, the candidates set up by the party have**secured not **less than six per cent of the total valid votes polled in the State**; and, in addition, the party has**returned at least one **member to the House of the People from that State at such general election. **Hence, the Political party is not eligible to be recognised as a State Political party in State B by ECI. **Knowledge Base:**Paragraph 6A of the Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968 **provides the following conditions for recognition as a State Party-A political party shall be eligible for**recognition as a State party**in a State, if, and only if, any of the following conditions is fulfilled: At the last general election to the Legislative Assembly of the State, the candidates set up by the party have secured**not less than six per cent of the total valid votes**polled in the State; and, in addition, the party has returned**at least two members to the Legislative Assembly of that State at such general election**At the last general election to the House of the People from that State, the candidates set up by the party have**secured not less than six per cent of the total valid votes polled in the State**; and, in addition, the party has**returned at least one member to the House of the People from that State at such general election**At the last general election to the Legislative Assembly of the State, the** party has won at least three per cent of **the total number of seats in the Legislative Assembly**At the last general election to the House of the People from the State, the party has returned**at least one **member to the House of the People for every 25 member**s At the last general election to the House of the People from the State, or at the last general election to the Legislative Assembly of the State, the candidates set up by the Party have**secured not less than eight per cent of **the total valid votes polled in the State.**Paragraph 6B of the Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968 **provides the following conditions for recognition as a National Party-A political party shall be eligible to be recognised as a National party if and only if any of the following conditions is fulfilled: I. The candidates set up by the party, in any**four or more States**, at the last general election to the House of the People, or to the Legislative Assembly of the State concerned, have**secured not less than six percent of the total **valid votes polled in each of those States at that general election; and, in addition, it has returned at least four **members to the House of the People at the aforesaid last general election from any State or States**II. At the last general election to the House of the People, the**party has won at least two per cent of the total **number of seats in the House of the People**and the party's**candidates have been elected to that House from not **less than three States**III. The party is**recognised as State party in at least four States**

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: Overseas Indians can exercise franchise in an election to the Lok Sabha under which of the following conditions?
1. They must be citizens of India.
2. Their names must figure in the electoral roll.
3. They must be present in India to vote.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.questionText: Overseas Indians can exercise franchise in an election to the Lok Sabha under which of the following conditions? 1. They must be citizens of India. 2. Their names must figure in the electoral roll. 3. They must be present in India to vote. Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**

Statement 1 is correct
- **An overseas elector must be a citizen of India to be eligible for voting in the Lok Sabha elections.**

Statement 2 is correct
- **The name of the overseas elector must be included in the electoral roll of the constituency they would be voting from to be eligible to vote.**

Statement 3 is correct
- **Overseas Indian voters are required to be physically present in India for voting, as e-voting is not allowed. They have the options of postal ballots and proxy voting, with certain restrictions and eligibility criteria.

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: Consider the following:
I. Giving a gift or an offer by a candidate to an elector to vote or refrain from voting at an election.
II. Any direct or indirect interference on the part of the candidate by threatening an elector, or any person in whom the elector is interested, with social ostracism.
III. Booth capturing by the candidate.
IV. The appeal by a candidate to vote or refrain from voting for any person on the ground of his/her religion.
How many of the above are considered corrupt practices under the Representation of the People Act, 1951?
questions.questionText: Consider the following: I. Giving a gift or an offer by a candidate to an elector to vote or refrain from voting at an election. II. Any direct or indirect interference on the part of the candidate by threatening an elector, or any person in whom the elector is interested, with social ostracism. III. Booth capturing by the candidate. IV. The appeal by a candidate to vote or refrain from voting for any person on the ground of his/her religion. How many of the above are considered corrupt practices under the Representation of the People Act, 1951?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Section 123 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, defines "corrupt practices" in elections, including **bribery, undue influence, appeals based on religion, caste, community or language, promotion of enmity, false **statements about a candidate's character, excessive election expenditure, hiring vehicles to transport voters, **seeking assistance from government servants, and booth capturing. **These acts undermine electoral fairness and can invalidate a candidate's election.** Option I is correct: **Under**Section 123(1)**, "**any gift, offer or promise of any gratification**to induce an elector to vote or refrain from voting" constitutes**bribery**, which is a corrupt practice.**Thus, giving a gift or an offer by a candidate or his/her agent to an elector to vote or refrain from voting at an **election will be considered as corrupt practices under Section 123 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951. ** Option II is correct: **Under**Section 123(2)**, threatening an elector with**"injury of any kind"**to influence voting is "**undue influence**," and is expressly defined as a corrupt practice.**Thus, any direct or indirect interference on the part of the candidate by threatening an elector, or any person in **whom the elector is interested, with social ostracism**will be considered as corrupt practices under Section 123 of **the Representation of the People Act, 1951.** Option III is correct: **According to**Section 123(8)**, Booth capturing by a candidate or his agent or other person is a corrupt practice.**Thus, booth capturing by a candidate or his agent will be considered as corrupt practices under Section 123 of the **Representation of the People Act, 1951.** Option IV is correct: **Under**Section 123(3)**, any**"appeal by a candidate to vote or refrain from voting for any person **on the ground of his religion, race, caste, community or language or the use of, or appeal to religious symbols"**is a corrupt practice.**Thus, the appeal by a candidate or his agent to vote or refrain from voting for any person on the ground of his/her **religion will be considered as corrupt practices under Section 123 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951.**

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: With reference to the Representation of the People Act, 1950, consider the following subjects:
I. Allocation of seats in the House of People
II. Preparation and revision of electoral rolls for assembly constituencies
III. Notification for general election to the House of People
IV. Registration of political parties
How many of the above subjects are dealt with under the Representation of the People Act, 1950?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Representation of the People Act, 1950, consider the following subjects: I. Allocation of seats in the House of People II. Preparation and revision of electoral rolls for assembly constituencies III. Notification for general election to the House of People IV. Registration of political parties How many of the above subjects are dealt with under the Representation of the People Act, 1950?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The**Representation of the People Act, 1950 (RPA 1950)**is a key law enacted to regulate**India's electoral system**at both the Union and State levels. It provides for the**allocation of seats**in Parliament and State Legislatures,**delimitation of constituencies**,**qualifications and disqualifications of voters**,**preparation of electoral rolls**, and the**procedure for filling seats**.** Option I is correct: Representation of the People Act, 1950**deals with the allocation of seats in both the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies, as provided in**Section 3 of the act**, which**specifies the allocation of seats in the **House of the People**, and**Section 7 of the act**, which specifies the total number of seats for each State Legislative Assembly.** Option II is correct: Representation of the People Act, 1950**lays down the qualifications of voters and the procedure for preparing and revising electoral rolls. Part IIB deals with Electoral rolls for parliamentary constituencies and** PART **III deals with electoral rolls for assembly constituencies. Section 21 under Part III deals with Preparation and **revision of electoral rolls. ** Option III is incorrect: Representation of People Act 1951 under Part III deals with Notification Of General **Elections to the House of People. ** Option IV is incorrect: Registration of political parties**is also part of Section 29A of**RPA 1951**, not RPA 1950. The RPA 1950 Act does not deal with political parties at all.

questions.questionText: Consider the following institutions: I. Council of States II. Panchayats at the village level III. State Legislative Council IV. State Legislative Assembly In how many of the above given institutions, the Constitution of India provides reservations for Scheduled Tribes?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.** Option I is incorrect: **The Constitution provides for reservation of seats to be filled by direct elections in the House of the People (Lok Sabha) but**the similar reservation of seats to the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes are **not applicable to the Rajya Sabha. ** Option II is correct: **Article 243D of Indian Constitution (added by 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992),**provides for the reservation of seats for scheduled castes and scheduled tribes in every panchayat (i.e., at all the **three levels including Gram Panchayats) in proportion of their population to the total population in the panchayat **area. ** Option III is incorrect: **The Constitution provides for reservation of seats to be filled by direct elections in State Legislative Assemblies for the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes**. The similar reservation of seats to the **Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes are not applicable to the State legislative council. ** Option IV is correct: Article 330 of the Constitution provides for reservation of seats for those from Scheduled **Castes/Schedule Tribes in the House of the People. Similarly, Article 332 of the Constitution provides for **reservation of seats for those from SCs/STs in the state legislative assemblies. As **per Article 334, reservation of seats for the two communities will cease after expiry of the period prescribed under this provision. Initially, it put a cap of 10 years for the cessation of the reservation, which was periodically increased. The 104th Constitutional Amendment Act of 2019 extended the reservation of seats in Lok Sabha for Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs) until January 25, 2030.

questions.questionText: Which of the statements given below is correct regarding the special provisions related to Scheduled castes in the Constitution of India? services or posts in connection with the affairs of the Union can be lowered. People. Scheduled Castes prepared at the state level.

options.a: Part XV of the Indian Constitution provides special provisions for Scheduled Castes (SCs).
options.b: For the members of the Scheduled castes the evaluation standards with respect to reservation in matters of
options.c: From each State of India at least one seat shall be reserved for the members of Scheduled Caste in the House of
options.d: The President of India through a public notification can include in or exclude any caste, race or tribe from the list of

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer **

Option a is incorrect:** Part XVI of the Indian Constitution under Article 330 to 342A **does provide special provisions for**Scheduled Castes (SCs), Scheduled Tribes (STs), Backward Classes, and Anglo-Indians in India. **These provisions, among others, include**reservation of seats in Parliament and state legislatures, reservation of jobs in government **and public sector undertakings. **

Option b is correct: The 82nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2000 introduced a specific provision in Article 335, **allowing the government to relax qualifying marks and lower evaluation standards for SCs and STs in promotions. **As per Article 335 of the Indian Constitution, the State can make provision in favour of the members of the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes for relaxation in qualifying marks in any examination or lowering the standards of evaluation, for reservation in matters or promotion to any class or classes of services or posts in connection with the affairs of the Union or of a State.**

Option c is incorrect: **As per Article 330 of the Indian Constitution the number of seats reserved in any State or Union territory for the Scheduled Castes will be in the same proportion to the total number of seats allotted to that State or Union territory in the House of the People as the population of the Scheduled Castes in the State or Union territory.**Nowhere the Constitution mandates that each State of India at least one seat shall be reserved for the members of **Scheduled Caste in the House of People. **

Option d is incorrect: **As per Article 341(1) of the Indian Constitution the President in consultation with Governor of the respective state by public notification specify the castes, races or tribes or parts of or groups within castes, races or tribes which shall for the purposes of this Constitution be deemed to be Scheduled Castes in relation to that State.**Further Article 341(2) Parliament may by law include in or exclude from the list of Scheduled Castes specified in **a notification issued under clause (1) any caste, race or tribe or part of or group within any caste, race or tribe.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**.**

Statement I is incorrect**. As per the Multi State Co-operative Societies (Amendment) Act, 2023**Co-operative Election **Authority**consists of a Chairperson, a Vice-Chairperson and Members not exceeding three. The Authority shall discharge the following functions, namely - (i)**conduct of elections of the Multi State Co-operative Society**; (ii) supervise, direct and control the matters relating to preparation of electoral rolls; and (iii) such other functions as may be prescribed.**

Statement II is correct**.**Two seats for women **and one seat for Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe have been reserved in the Board of a multi-State co-operative society**as per the MSCS (Amendment) Act, 2023.**

Statement III is correct**. In India,**the minimum number of members required to form a cooperative society is ten. **

questions.questionText: Consider the following: I. Speaker of the House of the People II. Deputy Speaker of the House of the People III. Deputy Chairman of the Council of States IV. questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer **As per the tenth schedule of the Indian Constitution, **a person who has been elected to the** office of the Speaker or **the Deputy Speaker of the House of the People or the Deputy Chairman of the Council of States or the Chairman or **the Deputy Chairman of the Legislative Council of a State **or the Speaker or the Deputy Speaker of the Legislative Assembly of a State, shall not be disqualified on the ground of defection under the tenth schedule of the Indian Constitution-if he/she, by reason of his election to such office,**voluntarily gives up the membership of the political party**to which he/she belonged immediately before such election and does not,**so long as he/she continues to hold such **office thereafter, rejoin that political party or become a member of another political party. **

questions.subject: Polity
statementLines: Which one of the following statements about the National Capital Territory (NCT) of Delhi is incorrect? mentioned in the State List of the seventh schedule of the Indian Constitution. President on the advice of the Chief Minister.
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements about the National Capital Territory (NCT) of Delhi is incorrect? mentioned in the State List of the seventh schedule of the Indian Constitution. President on the advice of the Chief Minister.

options.a: The Legislative Assembly for the NCT cannot make laws on subjects related to police, public order, and land
options.b: The Constitution of India fixes the number of seats for the Legislative Assembly for the NCT to 70.
options.c: Parliament can make a law to repeal any law made by the Legislative Assembly for the NCT on any matter.
options.d: The Chief Minister of NCT of Delhi is appointed by the President, and the other ministers are appointed by the

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. The governance of Delhi is based on**Article 239AA**, inserted by the**69th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1991**, which grants Delhi a Legislative Assembly with limited state-like powers. Its structure, powers, and relationship with the Union Government are clearly defined in the Constitution.**Statement a is correct**. The Legislative Assembly of National Capital Territory**cannot legislate**on three subjects of the State List of the seventh schedule of the Indian Constitution which are**Public order**(Entry 1),**Police**(Entry 2),**Land**(Entry 18). These exclusions come directly from**Article 239AA(3)a)**.**Statement b is incorrect**. The Constitution**does not fix a specific size**for the Delhi Legislative Assembly. Instead, Article 239AA only**creates**the Assembly; the**actual number of seats**is determined by**Parliament through law**, not by the Constitution itself. The current strength (70 seats) comes from the**Government of National Capital Territory of **Delhi Act, 1991**, not the Constitution.**Statement c is correct**. Under**Article 239AA(3)c), **If there is any inconsistency between a Delhi Assembly law and a parliamentary law, the parliamentary law prevails, unless the law passed by the Delhi Assembly has received the President's assent.**But at the same time Parliament can enact at any time any law with respect to the same matter including a law **adding to, amending, varying or repealing the law so made by the Legislative Assembly. **Statement d is correct**. As per**Article 239AA(5)**, the**Chief Minister of Delhi is appointed by the President**, and the**other ministers are appointed by the President on the Chief Minister's advice**. These ministers form the**Council of **Ministers**, which is responsible for the**day-to-day administration**of the National Capital Territory. This**Council aids **and advises the Lieutenant Governor (LG)**in the exercise of his functions. While Delhi has an elected government, the**LG remains the constitutional head**, reflecting Delhi's**special administrative status**.

statementLines: In the context of Indian Polity, consider the following:
I. Election of the members of the Council of States
II. Election to the office of the Vice President of India
III. Election of the members of the State Legislative Councils
IV. Election of the members of Municipal Corporations
V. Election to the office of the President of India The Election Commission of India (ECI) is responsible for administering elections for
how many of the above?
questions.questionText: In the context of Indian Polity, consider the following: I. Election of the members of the Council of States II. Election to the office of the Vice President of India III. Election of the members of the State Legislative Councils IV. Election of the members of Municipal Corporations V. Election to the office of the President of India The Election Commission of India (ECI) is responsible for administering elections for how many of the above?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The**Election Commission of India**(ECI), established under**Article 324**of the Constitution, is entrusted with the**superintendence, direction and control of elections to Parliament, State Legislatures, **and the**offices of the **President and Vice-President of India.**However, elections to local bodies such as Municipalities and Panchayats are conducted separately by the State Election Commissions (SECs), created under the 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendments.** Option I is correct: Elections of the members of the Council of States-Conducted by the ECI **Under Article 324 of the Constitution, the**superintendence, direction and control of these elections rest **exclusively with the Election Commission of India**(ECI). ECI issues the notification, supervises the voting, handles nominations, scrutiny, counting, and declaration of results.** Option II is correct: Election to the office of the Vice President of India-Conducted by ECI **Article 324 and relevant provisions of the**Presidential and Vice-Presidential Elections Act, 1952**and**the **Presidential and Vice-Presidential Election Rules, 1974**, vest the superintendence, direction and control of the election to the office of the Vice-President of India**in the ECI.**Thus,**ECI has full charge of this election.** Option III is correct: Election of the members of the State Legislative Councils-Conducted by ECI **Legislative Councils are part of the State Legislature. Their elections fall under Article 324, which**also vests in **the**ECI **the**powers of superintendence, direction and control of **the** elections to both Houses of **the**State Legislature**(**Legislative Assembly + Legislative Council**). The electorate includes: Local bodies' members, Graduates, Teachers, MLAs of the respective state** Option IV is incorrect: Election of the members of Municipal Corporations-Not conducted by ECI **Municipal Corporations and Municipalities are part of Urban Local Government, defined under**Part IX-A**of the Constitution**(Articles 243P-243ZG**). Article 243K gives the**power of superintendence, direction and control of municipal elections to**the**State **Election Commission**(SEC),**not the ECI.** Option V is correct: Election to the office of the President of India-Conducted by ECI **Article 324**and provisions of the**Presidential and Vice-Presidential Elections Act, 1952**and**the Presidential and **Vice-Presidential Election Rules, 1974**, vest the superintendence, direction and control of the election to the office of the President of India**in the ECI. **
